title: Practice problem - Chef and Happy String

content.problemStatement:
Chef has a string SSS with him consisting of only lowercase alphabets.

Chef is happy if the string contains a **contiguous substring** of length **strictly greater** than 222 in which all its characters are vowels. 

Determine whether Chef is happy or not. 

**Note**: in english alphabet, vowels are `a`, `e`, `i`, `o`, and `u`.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains of a single line of input, a string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, if Chef is happy, print `HAPPY` else print `SAD`.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single string S consisting only of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "HAPPY" if there exists a contiguous substring of length strictly greater than 2 consisting only of vowels (a, e, i, o, u). Otherwise output "SAD".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: the whole string "aeiou" is a vowel substring of length 5 (>2) → HAPPY.
Test case 2: no three consecutive vowels → SAD.
Test case 3: vowels are isolated, never three in a row → SAD.
Test case 4: substring "eea" (positions 4‑6) consists only of vowels and has length 3 → HAPPY.
First string "aei" itself is three vowels → HAPPY. Second string never has three consecutive vowels → SAD.
The string contains no vowel at all, therefore no qualifying substring exists.

solution.approach: Traverse the string while maintaining a counter of the current length of a consecutive vowel segment. Whenever the current character is a vowel, increment the counter; otherwise reset it to zero. If the counter ever reaches 3 (i.e., a substring of length >2), we can immediately conclude the answer is HAPPY for that test case. If the end of the string is reached without the counter reaching 3, the answer is SAD. This runs in linear time per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|S|) per test case, overall O(∑|S|) which is at most O(T·100).
